SnowDistribution: Deep snow - hard going on foot above 600m. A day of flat light and no definition. Winds 30 -40 mph, progress difficult and tiring, blowing us over on occasions
Comment: Freezing level forecast to rise to 1600m by 5pm accompanied by rain.
WeatherInfluences: Persistent snow showers throughout the period turning to rain at all levels late in the day. Cold overnight but temperatures progressively easing during the morning then much milder later. Strong West South West later South West winds.
SnowStability: Poor stability on very steep North to South East aspects above 950m during the morning, and again very late in the period with the onset much milder weather and rain at all levels. All recently formed cornices will be weak and prone to sudden collapse. Better stability on less steep ground, lower altitudes and all wind exposed locations.  The avalanche hazard will be Considerable
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Cold overnight with snow showers. Milder for a time during the morning with rain at most levels. Cooler later with further snow showers. Strong South West later West South West winds.
ObservedSnowStability: Period of poor stability in the morning with cornice triggered avalanche activity on a steep East South Easterly aspect at 1000m. Some early consolidation in all snow later in the morning followed by persistent drifting and new windslab development on steep North to South East aspects above 950m. Weak new cornices building over lee slopes. Better stability in all locations below 850m.  The avalanche hazard is Considerable

SnowDistribution: Wind scoured bare and icy on some aspects. No snow below around 500m. Whiteout conditions with poor visibility above 900m.  Winds 20-30 mph progress difficult at times with stronger gusts.
Comment:
Another day of unsettled weather for tomorrow with a rising freezing level towards the end of the forecast period. 
Improving forecast from midweek.
WeatherInfluences: Further snow showers will be occasionally heavy into the evening and overnight, turning lighter and fewer from late morning. Snow will fall initially above around 400 metres then gradually turn to rain as the freezing level rises above the summits by the end of the day. Very strong to gale force South-Westerly winds.
SnowStability: Localised areas of windslab will continue to develop overnight and into the morning with an enhanced period of instability during the afternoon as the freezing level rises. Steep wind sheltered areas such as gullies and coire rims on North through East to South-East aspects above 800 metres will be most affected. Remaining cornices will be prone to collapse. Elsewhere there will be a shallow covering of soft stable snow. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Showery precipitation was occasionally heavy, falling as rain for a time to the higher summits during the morning then turning to snow and hail above 500 metres. Fresh South-Westerly winds. The freezing level was around 600 metres.
ObservedSnowStability: Localised areas of moderately bonded windslab are present in some steep wind sheltered locations. North through East to South-East aspects above 800 metres are most affected. Elsewhere there is a thin covering of stable snow. The avalanche hazard is Moderate.

SnowDistribution: Deep snow in drifts with exposed areas scoured and icy. Poor visibility above 1000 metres and in showers. Winds 30 - 40 mph making progress difficult.
Comment: The freezing level is expected to rise above the summits late on Monday afternoon and remain there through Tuesday.
WeatherInfluences: There will be gale force South-Westerly winds and snow showers. These will turn to rain at gradually higher elevations during the afternoon. The freezing level will be around 400 metres overnight, rising through the day to above summit height by the end of the period.
SnowStability: Unstable windslab will be present and continue to develop through the period mainly on North-West through North to South-East aspects above 800 metres. Fragile cornices will be present above these aspects. There will be a period of instability towards the end of the afternoon when wet snow instabilities will develop. Avalanches and cornice collapse will be likely at this time. Windward aspects will remain stable. The avalanche hazard will be Considerable.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: There were very strong West-South-Westerly winds and snow showers to increasingly low levels through the day. The freezing level was dropping, around 700 metres at midday.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack has consolidated slightly in the milder overnight conditions but instabilities persist in windslab mainly on North-West through North to South-East aspects above 900 metres. Slopes exposed to the strong winds remain firm and icy. Cornices are fragile and prone to collapse. The avalanche hazard is Considerable.

SnowDistribution: Very hard snow ice on exposed windward slopes. Blizzard and whiteout conditions aloft presenting challenging and serious conditions. Bright spells at lower elevations. Winds approx 40-50 mph making progress very difficult and strenuous.
Comment: Windslab will be deposited throughout the period. Be alert to rising temperatures and the potential for a period of instability at the end of the day.
WeatherInfluences: Precipitation is expected throughout the forecast period. Initially this will fall as snow above 400 metres overnight, gradually turning to rain, with snow confined to above 800 metres in the afternoon. Winds will be gale force and from the West-South-West. The freezing level will be around 600 metres rising to 1800 metres by the end of the day.
SnowStability: Unstable windslab will continue to be deposited in steep wind sheltered areas such as gully tops, coire rims and crag aprons. The greatest instability will be present on North-East and East aspects above 950 metres, with additional cross loaded deposits on North and South-East aspects. Elsewhere the snowpack will be stable with a very firm and icy surface. Cornices will be fragile, particularly as temperatures rise at the end of the day. The avalanche hazard will be Considerable.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Snow fell overnight followed by snow showers during the day above 700 metres. Winds were gale to storm force South-South-Westerly during the day. The freezing level was around 800 metres, rising briefly to summit level in the early hours of the morning.
ObservedSnowStability: Unstable windslab continued to accumulate throughout the period on North to East aspects above 950 metres. Steep wind sheltered areas such as gully tops and coire rims are affected, with additional cross loaded deposits present on North-West to South-East aspects. Avalanche debris was present on these aspects in the morning. New fresh cornices are fragile and present the threat of collapse, while wind exposed slopes are very firm and icy. The avalanche hazard is Considerable.

SnowDistribution: Wet snowpack with fresh snow accumulations forming.  Cloudy on the tops with some bright spells, poor visibility during showers. Winds approx. 20mph with stronger gusts.
Comment: Wet overnight on Monday into Tuesday.
WeatherInfluences: Showers continue through the period, initially falling as snow above 300 metres this evening and then to increasingly higher elevations as the freezing level rises, going above the summits during the afternoon with rain at all elevations. Winds will be strong to gale force from mainly the South-West.
SnowStability: Existing moderately bonded windslab will be present with fresh accumulations of unstable windslab forming overnight and during the day. Areas affected will be in steep wind sheltered locations mainly on North-West through North to East aspects above 850 metres. Other aspects may be affected through crossloading. Cornices will be fragile on these aspects also. At the end of the period, as temperatures rise, there will be increased wet snow instabilities. The avalanche hazard will be Considerable.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: A damp morning with rain and snow, falling above 600 metres, becoming showery with some brighter intervals into the afternoon. Winds were fresh to strong from the South-West.
ObservedSnowStability: The existing snowpack has thawed and consolidated at all elevations becoming moist and generally soft, improving stability. Moderately bonded windslab remains in deeper accumulations which are mainly in steep locations on North-West through North to East aspects above 850 metres. Cornices remain on these aspects also. Fresh snow accumulations are building above 600 metres on most aspects with a thin layer of soft windslab in sheltered locations above 850 metres. The avalanche hazard is Moderate.

SnowDistribution: Very patchy snow below 800m, better cover above 900m. Cloud base initially at 900m, lifting above the summits later. Winds approx 40-50 mph making progress very difficult and strenuous at high summit level.
Comment: If heading into the main Cairngorms from the south, have a read of the North Cairngorms report. More new snow in the mountains north of the River Dee with a rapid freezing level rise expected during the afternoon.
WeatherInfluences: Very light snowfall above 500 metres overnight. Continuing through the day at increasingly higher altitudes only. Strong West-South-Westerly winds until midday, backing South-South-Westerly during the afternoon. 500 metre freezing level overnight, rising after day break to reach 1150 metres by the end of the period.
SnowStability: Moderately bonded windslab will be present in wind sheltered locations on North-West through North-East to South-East aspects above 900 metres. Steeper locations such as gully tops and sharply defined corrie rims will be most affected. Small fragile cornices will be present. Old snow will refreeze overnight, possessing firm surface layers by morning. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Light snow showers during the morning, brighter weather in the afternoon. Gale force Southerly winds veering Westerly. Freezing level fluctuating between 850 and 950 metres.
ObservedSnowStability: Steeper wind sheltered locations on North-West through North-East to South-East aspects above 900 metres are holding moderately bonded deposits. Gully tops and sharply defined corrie rims are most affected. Fresh accumulations continue to form due to redistribution. Wind exposed locations are scoured and firm. Deposits below 800 metres are thawing. The avalanche hazard is Moderate.
